coredata.dc:title: NIR light-controlled mitochondria-targeted delivery of carbon monoxide combined with histone deacetylase inhibition for synergistic anticancer therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Qi
Yu, Ya-Ting
Zhang, Hai-Lin
Wang, Yi
Liu, Jing
Yang, Shi-Ping
Liu, Jin-Gang
coredata.dc:description: A multifunctional nanoplatform APIPB–MnCO@TPP@N,P-GQDs (APIPB = N-(2-aminophen-yl)-4-(1H-imidazo[4,5-f] [1, 10] phenanthrolin-2-yl) benzamide, TPP = triphenylphosphine, Mn = manganese, CO = carbon monoxide, and GQDs = graphene quantum dots), nanoplatform (1), was synthesized, which consists of a fluorescent N, P-doped GQDs carrier with its surface covalently functionalized by an CO donor APIPB–MnCO with histone deacetylases (HDAC) inhibitory property and a TPP derivative directing group. Nanoplatform (1) selectively localized in the mitochondria of HeLa cells to inhibit HDAC activity, and released CO upon 808 nm near-infrared light irradiation, destroying the mitochondria and thus inducing cancer cells apoptosis. The targeted subcellular mitochondrial CO delivery combined with inhibitory HDAC activity maximized the cytotoxicity of the nanoplatform which may provide new insights for CO-mediated multimodal therapies for cancer treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Manganese carbonyl complexes
Carbon monoxide
Light-controlled release
Histone deacetylase inhibitor
Graphene quantum dots nanoplatform

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro delivery to mitochondria of HeLa cells
Intracellular CO and mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of nanoplatform (1) showing the damage it causes to mitochondria and the inhibition of the HDAC of cancer cells.

(A) TEM image of nanoplatform (1), with the inset showing the statistical distribution of its particle size; (B) FT-IR spectra; (C) UV–vis DRS of the N,P-GQDs (black), TPP (green), APIPB-MnCO (blue) and nanoplatform (1) (red); and the (D) fluorescence spectra of the N,P-GQDs (black) and nanoplatform (1) (red). (E) XPS survey spectrum and (F) High-resolution Mn 2p spectrum of nanoplatform (1). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Changes in the absorbance of Hb in the presence of nanoplatform (1) irradiated by an 808 nm laser (800 mW/cm2), with the inset showing the CO release profiles. (B) The NIR controllability of nanoplatform (1) for CO release upon the on/off switching of an 808 nm laser. (C) FT-IR spectral changes of a solid sample of nanoplatform (1) under an 808 nm laser. (D) Changes in the fluorescence of the CO probe system (FL-CO-1 + PdCl2) in the presence of nanoplatform (1) irradiated under an 808 nm laser (800 mW/cm2).

Confocal laser fluorescence microscopy images of HeLa cells treated with the (A) mitochondria-targeted nanoplatform (1) or (B) non-targeted APIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs co-stained with MitoTracker Green. The ROI intensity graphs reveal the fluorescence intensity profiles across the cells. Scale bars: 50 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence detection of mitochondria membrane potential (MMP) of the APIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs and nanoplatform (1)-treated HeLa cells (A) in the dark and (B) under 808 nm laser irradiation. Scale bars: 100 μm.

MTT cytotoxicity assays of HeLa cells treated with the different nanoplatforms at concentrations of 0–80 μg/mL; (A) N,P-GQDs in the dark; (B) PIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs and APIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs;(C) APIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs; and nanoplatform (1) in the dark or irradiated under an 808 nm laser; and (D) comparison of the cell viability of HeLa cells incubated with 80 μg/mL of the PIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs, APIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs and nanoplatform (1) in the presence or absence of 808 nm laser irradiation (800 mW/cm2, 10 min) (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.005).

head.ce:title: NIR light-controlled mitochondria-targeted delivery of carbon monoxide combined with histone deacetylase inhibition for synergistic anticancer therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials, School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials, School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Resource Chemistry of MOE & Shanghai Key Lab of Rare Earth Functional Materials, Shanghai Normal University, Shanghai 200234, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Resource Chemistry of MOE & Shanghai Key Lab of Rare Earth Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Resource Chemistry of MOE & Shanghai Key Lab of Rare Earth Functional Materials, Shanghai Normal University, Shanghai 200234, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A multifunctional nanoplatform APIPB–MnCO@TPP@N,P-GQDs (APIPB = N-(2-aminophen-yl)-4-(1H-imidazo[4,5-f] [1, 10] phenanthrolin-2-yl) benzamide, TPP = triphenylphosphine, Mn = manganese, CO = carbon monoxide, and GQDs = graphene quantum dots), nanoplatform (1), was synthesized, which consists of a fluorescent N, P-doped GQDs carrier with its surface covalently functionalized by an CO donor APIPB–MnCO with histone deacetylases (HDAC) inhibitory property and a TPP derivative directing group. Nanoplatform (1) selectively localized in the mitochondria of HeLa cells to inhibit HDAC activity, and released CO upon 808 nm near-infrared light irradiation, destroying the mitochondria and thus inducing cancer cells apoptosis. The targeted subcellular mitochondrial CO delivery combined with inhibitory HDAC activity maximized the cytotoxicity of the nanoplatform which may provide new insights for CO-mediated multimodal therapies for cancer treatment.

A multifunctional APIPB-MnCO@TPP@N,P-GQDs nanoplatform {APIPB = N-(2-aminophen-yl)-4-(1H-imidazo[4,5-f] [1, 10] phenanthrolin-2-yl) benzamide, TPP = triphenylphosphine and GQDs = graphene quantum dots} exhibited mitochondria-targeted CO delivery together with inhibition of histone deacetylases activity under 808 nm near IR light irradiation, showing the synergistic anticancer therapy.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Histones are one of the most important components of eukaryotic chromosomes, as their acetylated and deacetylated forms regulate gene expression via key transcription and modification. Histone acetylation weakens the binding of DNA and histones, and can promote the binding of transcription-related enzymes to promoters [1]. However, histone deacetylase (HDACs) will increase histone deacetylation [2–4]. In normal cells, the acetylation and deacetylation of histones are in a dynamic balance. When the balance is broken, it may lead to the occurrence of tumors. In cancer cells, over-expressed HDACs increase histone deacetylation and make histones bind tightly to deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), which is not conducive to the expression of tumor suppressor genes [5,6]. Histone deacetylase inhibitors (HDACIs), a new class of potential anti-tumor drugs [7], regulate the expression and stability of apoptosis and differentiation-related proteins by increasing the level of acetylation in specific regions of the chromatin, ultimately inducing cell apoptosis and differentiation [8–10]. Although HDACIs have been proven to exhibit good anticancer effects, most of them are only used to treat malignant tumors in the hematological system [11], with the impact that they have on solid tumors being relatively weak. Therefore, combining HDACIs with chemo [12–14]/radiation [15,16]/immune [17–19]/photodynamic [20,21]/nitric oxide (NO) therapy [22,23] presents an effective strategy to enhance their anticancer activity.

As an emerging gas molecule for use in therapeutic treatments, carbon monoxide (CO) has attracted widespread attention for the treatment of cancer, owing to its multifaceted regulation of cellular function and the tumor microenvironment [24]. CO targets mitochondrial activity in cancer cells and accelerates cellular respiration (including O2 consumption and the overproduction of reactive oxygen species (ROS)), adenosine triphosphate (ATP) depletion, mitochondrial collapse, and other proapoptotic effects [25]. Moreover, as CO can freely traverse various biological membranes and tumor interstitium [26], it holds great potential in complementing other anticancer treatments, such as photodynamic therapy (PDT) [27–30], sonodynamic therapy (SDT) [31], chemodynamic therapy (CDT) [32,33], chemotherapy [34], and photothermal therapy (PTT) [35]. However, the combination of CO with HDACIs for cancer therapy has been rarely reported. Recently, we reported a novel bifunctional complex [MnBr(CO)3(APIPB)] (APIPB = N-(2-aminophen-yl)-4-(1H-imidazo[4,5-f] [1, 10] phenanthrolin-2-yl)benzamide) that exhibits inhibitory HDAC activity and visible light-controlled CO delivery [36]. However, its lack of targeted CO delivery, small tissue permeability, and phototoxicity of visible light limit its therapeutic effect.

In this study, a multifunctional nanoplatform APIPB–MnCO@TPP@N, P-GQDs (TPP = triphenylphosphine, N,P-GQDs = nitrogen and phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dots), nanoplatform (1), is reported for near-infrared (NIR) light-controlled mitochondria-targeted CO delivery together with the inhibitory activity of an HDAC (Fig. 1 ), wherein a manganese carbonyl complex [MnBr(CO)3(APIPB)] with an HDACI moiety and a mitochondrial-targeting TPP derivative are covalently attached to the surface of up-conversion N,P-GQDs. When incubated with HeLa cells, nanoplatform (1) specifically localized in the mitochondria, wherein it instantly released CO under 808 nm laser irradiation together with the synergistic inhibition of HDAC, thus inducing cell apoptosis.

All reagents were used as purchased commercially without further purification unless otherwise noted. 4-(1H-imidazo[4,5-f][1,10]phenanthrolin-2-yl)-N-phenylbenzamide (PIPB), N-(2-aminophen-yl)-4-(1H-imidazo[4,5-f] [1, 10] phenanthrolin-2-yl)benzamide) (APIPB), [MnBr(CO)3(APIPB)] (APIPB-MnCO) and [MnBr(CO)3(PIPB)] (PIPB-MnCO) [36], (TPPCOOH) [37], N,P-GQDs [38] and fluorescent CO probe (FL-CO-1) [39] were synthesized according to the previously reported literatures.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) photos were taken on a JEOL JEM-2011 transmission electron microscope operating at 100 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) date were collected on a Thermo Escalab 250 XPS instrument equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (hν = 1486.6 eV). All binding energies were referenced to the C1s peak (284.6 eV) arising from adventitious carbon. FTIR spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (IRPrestige-21). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2600). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Horiba Fluoromax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fluorescence images were acquired by using a Leica TCS SP5 II inverted microscope with a Leica DMI 6000B confocal scanning system.

4-Picolinic acid (IA) (307 mg, 2.5 mmol) and TPPCOOH (161 mg, 0.37 mmol) were dissolved in 2 mL DMF, and were activated by 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC)/N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) at room temperature for 1 h. Afterwards 2 mL aqueous solution of N, P-GQDs was added, and the mixture was stirred at 50 °C for 24 h. After that, NaHCO3 was added to adjust the pH of the solution to 6.0. Following that, the solution was dialyzed in dialysis tube (molecular weight cutoff 1000 Da) against DI water for 48 h. IA@TPP@N, P-GQDs was finally obtained by freeze drying.

[MnBr(CO)3(APIPB)] (163 mg, 0.25 mmol) and AgCF3SO3 (67 mg, 0.26 mmol) were dissolved in 8 mL DMF, and the mixture was stirred at 50 °C under dark condition for 6 h. After cooled to room temperature, the gray AgBr precipitate was removed by filtration, 40 mL deionized water was added to precipitate the final product. The solid precipitate was filtered, washed with deionized water and Et2O, dried in vacuo to obtain yellow product [Mn (CF3SO3)(CO)3(APIPB)].
The control sample of [Mn(CF3SO3)(CO)3(PIPB)] was prepared in a similar way by substitute [MnBr (CO)3(APIPB)] with [MnBr (CO)3(PIPB)].

The DMF solution of [Mn(CF3SO3)(CO)3(APIPB)] was added to the aqueous solution of IA@TPP@N,P-GQDs, and the mixed solution was stirred at 50 °C under the dark condition for 9 h. After that, the solution was dialyzed in dialysis tube (molecular weight cutoff 1000 Da) against DI water for 48 h. Nanoplatform (1) was finally obtained by freeze drying. XPS: Mn 6.59 wt%. FT-IR (KBr, cm−1): 3423 (s), 3114 (s), 2034 (s), 1934 (s), 1627 (m), 1503 (m), 1456 (m), 1402 (m), 1319 (w), 1261 (m), 1153 (w), 1030 (s), 860 (w), 810 (m), 758 (w), 710 (w), 638 (m), 528 (w). UV–vis DRS (λ max, nm): 212, 288, 337.
The control sample of PIPB-MnCO@N,P-GQDs and APIPB-MnCO@N,P-GQDs were obtained similar to that of nanoplatform (1) in the absence of TPPCOOH.
Cell culture and 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay were performed using the method we previously reported [36].
Extracellular and Intracellular CO detection were performed using the method we previously reported [36].

After the HeLa cells were pre-incubated with APIPB-MnCO@TPP@N,P-GQDs or nontargeted APIPB-MnCO@N,P-GQDs (30 μg/mL) for 4 h, the cells were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) three times, then incubated with Mito-Tracker Green (200 nM) for 30 min. Subsequently, the cells were washed with PBS buffer twice and imaged with serum-free Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM). Excitation was carried out with lasers at λ = 405 nm and 488 nm, and emissions were recorded in the range of 425–480 nm and 500–550 nm, respectively.

After the HeLa cells were pre-incubated with APIPB-MnCO@TPP@N,P-GQDs or nontargeted APIPB-MnCO@N,P-GQDs (30 μg/mL) for 4 h, the cells were washed with PBS buffer three times and irradiated by 808 nm laser (1.0 W/cm2) for 0 or 10 min. The cells were further incubated 12 h and then incubated with 5,5',6,6'-tetrachloro-1,1',3,3'-tetraethyl-benzimidazolylcarbocyanine iodide (JC-1,12.5 μM) for 20 min. Subsequently, cells were washed with JC-1 buffer twice and imaged with serum-free DMEM medium. Excitation was carried out with lasers at λ = 488 nm and 525 nm, and emissions were recorded in the wavelength range of 510–550 nm (JC-1 monomers) and 570–610 nm (JC-1 aggregates), respectively. All the parameters were kept constant in each sample.

Nanoplatform (1) was prepared according to the procedures shown in Scheme 1 , isonicotinic acid (IA) and TPP were first covalently functionalized to N, P-GQDs by forming an amide bond, and then through coordination with the nitrogen atom from IA to further anchor the CO donor [Mn(CF3SO3)(CO)3(APIPB)] with HADC inhibitory effect. The morphology of nanoplatform (1) was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In the TEM images, nanoplatform (1) is made up of spherical nanoparticles that have an average size of 5–6 nm (Fig. 2A), which are slightly larger than those of the non-targeted APIPB–MnCO@N, P-GQDs (Fig. S1A). The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of nanoplatform (1) is mainly dominated by the signals of the N,P-GQDs, with additional peaks centered at 3114 cm−1 corresponding to the C-H stretching vibration of the benzene group in the TPP moiety, as well as peaks at 2034 and 1934 cm−1, which can be assigned to the C-O stretching vibration of the terminal CO ligands in the APIPB–MnCO fragment (Fig. 2B) [40]. These vibrations are shifted to slightly lower wavenumbers compared with those of the uncoupled complex APIPB–MnCO (2038, 1942 cm−1, Fig. 2B), suggesting the successful covalent grafting of the CO donor onto the surface of the N,P-GQDs.

The ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectrum (UV–vis DRS) of nanoplatform (1) is similar to that of APIPB-MnCO. The peak at approximately 288 nm is related to the APIPB-MnCO moiety, which shifts to longer wavelength relative to that of the uncoupled compound (281 nm) (Fig. 2C). These results clearly demonstrate that the surface of the N,P-GQDs is covalently functionalized with APIPB–MnCO and TPP. This covalent functionalization of the N,P-GQDs results in a dramatic decrease in their fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2D). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) reveal the presence of C, N, O, P, and Mn on the surface of nanoplatform (1) (Fig. 2E), where the Mn content was determined to be 6.59 wt%, corresponding to 3.16 μmol of CO per milligram of nanoplatform (1). The Mn 2p XPS spectrum of nanoplatform (1) could be deconvolved into two sets of peaks, with one group at 640.8 and 652.7 eV, assignable to Mn–N-type Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2, and the other group at 642.1 and 653.9 eV corresponding to the Mn–C-type Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 2F) [41].

The NIR laser-triggered release of CO from nanoplatform (1) was assessed via hemoglobin (Hb) assay [42]. The UV–vis spectrum of Hb solution containing nanoplatform (1) exhibits a prominent Soret band at 430 nm, the intensity of which gradually decreases and shifts to 420 nm upon irradiation of the solution using an 808 nm laser, and at the same time, two Q-bands at 540 and 570 nm develop (Fig. 3A). This indicates that HbCO forms and around 80% of CO is released from nanoplatform (1) within 40 min. It should be noted that no CO leaching is observed in the dark, suggesting the good stability of the nanoplatform (1) in solution in the dark. In addition, NIR light-controlled CO release from nanoplatform (1) can be confirmed by the switching on/off of an 808 nm laser. When the laser is turned on, CO is immediately released, with its release instantly stopping once the laser is turned off (Fig. 3B). The intensity of νCO in the FT-IR spectrum of nanoplatform (1) in the solid state is gradually reduced after irradiation (Fig. 3C), which further verifies that the 808 nm laser triggers CO release from nanoplatform (1). CO production was also confirmed using a fluorescent probe (FL-CO-1), the fluorescence of which is enhanced in the presence of CO. [39] A probe solution of FL-CO-1 containing nanoplatform (1) shows relatively weak fluorescence at 513 nm in the dark, the intensity of which gradually increases over time under an 808 nm laser (Fig. 3D). To note that the CO release rate from nanoplatform (1) in the PBS solution is significantly higher than that of the solid state, which could be attributed to the solvent molecules coordination to the Mn metal center that promoted its CO releasing under NIR light irradiation [43]. These results indicate that CO released from nanoplatform (1) can be totally controlled using an 808 nm laser, which is beneficial to the spatiotemporal delivery of CO to the desired site to maximize its therapeutic effect and reduce the risk of CO poisoning. In addition, after nanoplatform (1) was kept in PBS buffer solution for 120 h in the dark (Fig. S2), its UV–vis absorption spectrum kept unchanged, indicating its good stability under physiological conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro delivery to mitochondria of HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: Before co-incubating with HeLa cells, the dispersion stability of nanoplatform (1) was first tested, where a good dispersion of nanoplatform (1) in both PBS solution (pH 7.4) and DMEM solution was observed (Fig. S3). Subsequently, the distribution of nanoplatform (1) in HeLa cells was examined via co-localization experiments, where the cells were co-incubated with nanoplatform (1) and the commercially available mitochondria-specific probe MitoTracker Green. It can be observed from the results that the blue fluorescence of the nanoplatform (1) matches well with the green fluorescence of the MitoTracker (Fig. 4A), indicating that the TPP-functionalized nanoplatform (1) group selectively accumulates in mitochondria and that, conversely, the control group APIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs lacking the TPP moiety is randomly distributed in the cytoplasm (Fig. 4B). The fluorescence intensity profiles of the regions of interest (ROIs) are also consistent with the above observations. In addition, co-localization staining experiment with nuclear-specific probe 4′, 6’-Diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) revealed that nanoplatform (1) can hardly reach the nucleus (Fig. S4). These results unambiguously indicate that TPP-functionalized nanoplatform (1) is capable of specifically targeting mitochondrial subcellular organelles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular CO and mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) detection
ce:section.ce:para: After demonstrating the specific mitochondrial localization of nanoplatform (1), the intracellular CO production was then evaluated. When HeLa cells are pre-incubated with nanoplatform (1) for 4 h and then incubated with the FL-CO-1/PdCl2 probe for 30 min, only very weak green fluorescence is observed in the absence of laser irradiation, and bright green fluorescence is observed under an 808 nm laser (Fig. S5). These results suggest that the 808 nm laser triggers the instant release of CO from nanoplatform (1) in mitochondria to maximize the therapeutic effects of CO and prevent its spontaneous release.

Although the mechanism is not yet completely clear, the mitochondrial dysfunction caused by CO could be the main factor in inducing cancer cell apoptosis [44]. Therefore, the mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) of HeLa cells was evaluated using a commercially available mitochondrial membrane potential dye JC-1, which produces red fluorescence (aggregated JC-1) at high MMP but green fluorescence (monomeric JC-1) at low MMP. As shown in Fig. 5A, after HeLa cells are incubated with nanoplatform (1) in the dark, the red fluorescence of JC-1 decreases and weak green fluorescence can be observed, which indicates that binding of nanoplatform (1) to the membrane combined with the retained HDAC inhibitory activity could result in the drop of membrane potential and thus of mitochondria destruction [45,46]. Under an 808 nm laser, the green fluorescence is significantly enhanced, suggesting that CO is released from nanoplatform (1), further reducing the MMP of the HeLa cells (Fig. 5B). It should be noted that owing to its specific accumulation in mitochondria, the nanoplatform (1)-treated cells experienced a much more significant MMP decrease than did those treated with the non-targeted APIPB–MnCO @N,P-GQDs nanoplatform.

The cytotoxicity of the nanoplatforms toward HeLa cells was assessed via standard MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 6A, the N, P-GQDs exhibit no toxicity toward HeLa cells in the dark over the tested concentration range of 10–80 μg/mL, suggesting the good biocompatibility of the N, P-GQDs. In contrast, the CO-releasing molecule-grafted nanoplatforms exhibit concentration dependent cytotoxicity in the dark, with the APIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs showing higher cytotoxicity than the PIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs nanoplatforms that lack the zinc-binding group (ZBG) (Fig. 6B). This may be due to the absence of the ZBG in PIPB–MnCO, which is thus incapable of inhibiting the HDAC of HeLa cells. And the lethality of the nanoplatforms toward HeLa cells significantly promoted under 808 nm laser irradiation. With the dosage of 80 μg/mL, the cell viability of APIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs-treated cells (34%) was much lower than the PIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs-treated ones (45%) upon 808 nm NIR light irradiation (Fig. 6B). It should be noted that the lethality of nanoplatform (1) toward HeLa cells is significantly higher than that of the non-targeted APIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs in both the absence and presence of 808 nm laser irradiation (Fig. 6C). Compared to the control samples, nanoplatform (1), which possesses both the mitochondria targeting group and ZBG, demonstrates the highest lethality toward HeLa cancer cells; and remarkable low cell viability (17%) of nanoplatform (1)-treated relative to that of the non-targetable-nanoplatform-treated (APIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs, 34%) and the none-ZBG-containing-nanoplatform-treated (PIPB–MnCO@N,P-GQDs, 45%) cells was found (Fig. 6D). This may be attributed to the selective accumulation of nanoplatform (1) in mitochondria, which maximizes the synergistic effect of HDAC inhibition and the release of CO to destroy the mitochondria in HeLa cells, thus leading to its higher efficacy. The crucial anticancer effect played by CO is further confirmed by comparing the cytotoxicity of the residue of nanoplatform (1) after CO-release by exhausting light illumination, where remarkably lower phototoxicity was observed for the CO-depleted residue (Fig. S6). This suggests that CO indeed significantly enhanced the cytotoxicity of nanoplatform (1).

In summary, a mitochondria-targeted multifunctional nanoplatform (1), which combines NIR laser-controlled CO release with HDAC inhibition, was prepared via the covalent attachment of the CO donor APIPB–MnCO with a ZBG and a TPP directing group on a fluorescent N,P-GQD carrier. Nanoplatform (1) is fluorescence-trackable and capable of targeting the mitochondria of cancer cells to which CO is delivered. Cytotoxicity assays reveal that nanoplatform (1) exhibits the highest lethality toward HeLa cells under NIR light irradiation compared with the control nanoplatforms that lack either the targeting group or the ZBG. In conclusion, the targeted subcellular mitochondrial CO delivery synergized with inhibitory HDAC activity may provide new insights for future CO-mediated multimodal therapies for the treatment of cancer.
This manuscript or its contents in other forms has not been published previously and/or is not under consideration for publication elsewhere at the time of submission.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no competing interests to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 21571062 to JGL), the Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at the Shanghai Institutions of Higher Learning to JGL, and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 222201717003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jinorgbio.2021.111656.
